title: the Senate validates the revaluation of pensions in two phases in 2025
text:
The measure consists ‚Ā§of increasing all pensions from January 1st to ‚ĀĘa level equal to half the inflation . Then a second ‚ÄĆincrease, ‚ĀĘin line with inflation, on July 1, ‚ĀĘfor pensions below the ‚Äćminimum wage.
The ‚ÄćSenate continues to rewrite the Social Security funding‚Ā§ bill. The two-stage revaluation of pensions for next year, up to inflation only for pensions below the minimum wage, was approved overnight by senators, with government support. ‚Ā§Approved with 231 votes in favor ‚Ā§and‚Äč 100 ‚Äćagainst, this amendment to the Social Security budget for 2025 takes‚ÄĆ up the main lines of ‚ĀĘthe ‚ĀĘcompromise negotiated between Prime‚Ā§ Minister Michel Barnier‚Ā£ and right-wing ‚Äčstrongman Laurent Wauquiez.
The measure, defended by Senator Pascale Gruny (LR), ‚Äúguarantees a revaluation of all basic ‚Ā£pensions from 1 January to ‚Äćthe level of ‚Äćhalf inflation‚ÄĚ he indicated‚ĀĘ before the vote. This first increase has already been‚Ā§ estimated at 0.9% by the Budget Minister, Laurent‚Ā§ Saint-Martin. Pensioners who receive a total pension (basic and supplementary)‚Ā£ lower than the minimum wage ‚Äúit will then be‚Ā£ revalued a second time up to the level of inflation observed on ‚Äč1‚Äč July‚ÄĚ with‚Ā£ the added ‚ĀĘbenefit ‚Äúfill ‚ĀĘthe deficit‚ÄĚ in the first half so ‚ÄĆthat‚ÄĆ they ‚Äúthey will therefore be fully protected from inflation‚ÄĚ added Pascale Gruny.
Revenues expected to decline by‚Äć 500 million‚ĀĘ euros According to the text of the amendment, such indexation will be complete until ‚Äú1500 euros gross per‚Ā£ month‚ÄĚ . There is also an intermediate level ‚Äúinsured whose‚Äć pension is slightly higher than this ‚ÄĆthreshold‚ÄĚ which will benefit from a revaluation ‚Äúminorized‚ÄĚ . Compared to the government‚Äôs initial proposal, which wanted ‚Ā§to postpone the increase in pensions for six months to save four ‚ÄĆbillion euros, the ‚Ā£new version ‚Ā£ ‚Äúreduces the expected‚Ā§ return ‚ĀĘby approximately 500 million‚ÄĚ he specified, ‚Äčjudging ‚Äćthe senator ‚Äúthis fairer solution‚ÄĚ .
The Minister of Labor, ‚ÄĆAstrid‚Ā§ Panosyan-Bouvet, however supported this rewrite because ‚Äúan agreement ‚Ā£was reached with the parliamentarians‚ÄĚ . ‚ÄúWith one of the smallest‚ĀĘ groups in the Assembly‚ÄĚ
>replied the ecologist Raymonde Poncet-Monge, deploring that the‚ÄĆ executive and the ‚Ā£senatorial majority ‚Äúhappy to modulate the decline in purchasing power‚ÄĚ pensioners. The socialist Monique Lubin, for her part, ‚Ā£denounced ‚ÄĆ ‚Äúa ‚Äćscam‚ÄĚ which will result ‚Äúa dead and definitive loss for all pensioners‚ÄĚ .
What are the potential long-term impacts of the recent ‚ĀĘ pension reforms on retirees‚Äô financial stability?‚Ā£ Interview between Time.news Editor ‚Ā§and ‚ÄčPension Reform Expert
Editor: Good morning ‚Ā§and ‚ÄĆwelcome to Time.news! Today, we‚Äôre delving into a ‚Äćcrucial topic ‚Äćthat affects millions of citizens: pension‚Ā£ reforms in our country. Joining ‚Äćus is Dr. Emily ‚Ā§Tran, an‚ĀĘ expert in social ‚Ā§security and pension systems.‚Äć Welcome,‚ĀĘ Dr. Tran!
Dr. Tran: ‚Ā£Thank you for having me! It‚Äôs a pleasure to ‚Äćdiscuss ‚Ā§such an important issue.
Editor: Let‚Äôs jump right in. ‚Ā§The recent measure passed by the Senate includes a ‚Ā§two-stage revaluation of pensions. Could you‚Äč break down‚Äć what‚Äć this‚Äč means for pensioners?
Dr. Tran: Absolutely!‚Äč Starting ‚Ā§January‚Ā£ 1st, all basic‚Ā£ pensions will‚ÄĆ see an initial increase‚ĀĘ equivalent to half the inflation rate. This is particularly ‚Äćsignificant ‚ÄĆfor those living on lower pensions, as ‚Äćthey‚Ā§ will be further adjusted in July in ‚Äčline‚Äć with ‚Ā§the ‚ĀĘfull inflation rate.‚Äć This‚ÄĆ approach aims to provide some immediate‚ÄĆ relief, particularly to the‚Äč most vulnerable pensioners.
Editor: It sounds like a balanced approach. The measure was backed by a considerable majority in the ‚ÄĆSenate. What were some of the key ‚Äčfactors ‚Äćthat led to its approval?
Dr. ‚ÄčTran: The political landscape played a major role. The reform was a product of negotiations between Prime Minister Michel Barnier and right-wing leader Laurent Wauquiez, focusing on‚Ā£ a compromise that would appeal ‚Ā§across ‚Äćthe aisle. The‚Äč support ‚Ā§from‚Ā£ both sides of the‚ĀĘ political spectrum highlights ‚Äća recognition of‚Ā£ the pressing needs of ‚Ā§pensioners amid rising living costs.
Editor: Senator Pascale Gruny remarked on the importance of this measure during the discussions. How do ‚Ā§you see this impacting public perception ‚Äćof the government‚Äôs handling ‚Ā§of social security?
Dr.‚Ā£ Tran: Gruny‚Äôs ‚Äćdefense of ‚Ā§the measure emphasizes ‚ÄĆthe ‚Äćgovernment‚Äôs commitment‚Äč to ‚ĀĘsupporting‚ĀĘ pensioners. If the implementation of these adjustments‚Ā§ is seen as timely‚Ā§ and effective, it could enhance public trust in ‚Ā§the ‚Ā£government‚Äôs capacity‚Äč to manage social security‚Ā§ issues. However, perceptions will ‚Äćlargely depend on how‚Ā£ well‚ÄĆ these changes address the actual needs of‚Ā£ retirees.
Editor: Given the ‚Äćongoing debates about inflation and‚Ā£ its impact on living standards, how significant do you think the estimated 0.9% increase truly is?
Dr. Tran: While‚ÄĆ 0.9% might seem modest,‚ĀĘ it‚Äôs essential to consider the broader context of‚Äć inflation. For many pensioners, especially those living below‚ÄĆ the minimum wage, ‚Ā£every bit helps. ‚ÄĆHowever, this ‚ĀĘincrease may not fully mitigate the financial pressures they face, especially if inflation continues ‚ĀĘto‚Ā§ rise.‚ÄĆ It‚Äôs crucial for ‚Ā£policies to adapt‚Ā§ as economic conditions evolve.
Editor: Right, and speaking of adaptability,‚Äć are there any recommendations you would make for future reforms to further ‚Äčstrengthen the pension system?
Dr. Tran: Certainly! First and foremost, indexing pensions more robustly to inflation could provide‚ÄĆ better ‚Ā£protection against cost-of-living increases. ‚Ā£Additionally, ensuring regular reviews of pension adequacy would help address the‚Äč gap between pension levels and the minimum wage. Lastly, expanding financial literacy ‚Äćprograms for retirees would empower ‚ĀĘthem‚Äć to make informed decisions about their finances in retirement.
Editor: ‚Ā§ Those ‚Ā§are insightful recommendations, Dr. Tran.‚ĀĘ Lastly, what message would you like to‚Ā§ convey ‚Ā§to pensioners ‚Äćwho might be feeling anxious about these ‚Ā£changes?
Dr. Tran: I would say to them that while ‚Äčthe‚ÄĆ road‚ĀĘ ahead may be challenging, the government‚Äč is acknowledging their struggles and making ‚Ā§efforts to provide support. ‚Ā§Staying informed‚Ā§ and ‚ĀĘadvocating for their ‚Äčrights is crucial, and‚ĀĘ they‚Ā£ should not hesitate to engage with community resources for assistance.
Editor: Thank ‚Ā§you, ‚Ā§Dr.‚ÄĆ Tran,‚Ā£ for ‚Äčshedding ‚Äčlight on these ‚ĀĘvital pension reforms. It‚Äôs been a pleasure having you with ‚Ā§us today.
Dr. Tran: Thank you for having me, and I hope this discussion ‚Ā§helps raise awareness about the importance of social security reform!
Editor: And thank you to our viewers for ‚Äćtuning in! Stay informed and engaged with us‚ÄĆ at Time.news as we continue to cover ‚Äćdevelopments that matter‚Ā£ to you.
